mnemonics.title: Characteristic Polynomial
mnemonics.content: CHARACTERISTIC: |A - λI| = 0. Remember: 'Det(A minus Lambda I) equals Zero' - think of 'A-Lambda Identity Makes Zero'

mnemonics.title: Eigen Properties
mnemonics.content: ALGEBRAIC: count how many times (multiplicity). GEOMETRIC: dimension of space (linearly independent vectors). AG ≤ GG (Algebraic ≥ Geometric). Remember: 'Algebraic Greater or Equal Geometric'

mnemonics.title: Diagonalization Condition
mnemonics.content: To diagonalize: need n independent eigenvectors. Sum of geometric multiplicities = n. Remember: 'Full Set Forms Diagonal' - full set of eigenvectors gives diagonal form

acronyms.usage: Every matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation p(A) = 0
A = PDP⁻¹ where D is diagonal matrix of eigenvalues and P contains eigenvectors
Extension of eigenvalue decomposition for non-square matrices: A = UΣVᵀ
For each eigenvalue λ: 1 ≤ geometric multiplicity ≤ algebraic multiplicity

visualTips.visualization: Imagine eigenspace as a line (1D), plane (2D), or full space (3D) passing through origin, where every vector gets scaled but not rotated when transformation is applied
Think of eigenvalues as 'fixed points' on the number line where det(A-λI) crosses zero - the graph of characteristic polynomial shows eigenvalues at x-intercepts
Algebraic = how many times root appears (count on polynomial). Geometric = dimension of eigenspace (number of independent direction arrows). For defective: geometric < algebraic

analogies.analogy: Like a rubber sheet being stretched - eigenvectors are directions that just get longer/shorter but don't rotate, while other directions twist and turn
It's like finding the 'resonant frequencies' of a system - just as a tuning fork vibrates at specific frequencies, matrices have specific eigenvalues that are 'natural' scales
Like choosing the right coordinate system - diagonalization is finding the 'natural' basis where the transformation acts simply as scaling along each axis
